prelimsPoints: Project Tiger: Launched in 1973 to protect tigers and their habitats.
India's share: Home to over 70% of the world's wild tigers.
Conservation methods: Anti-poaching, habitat restoration, community participation, scientific management.
Challenges: Human-wildlife conflict, habitat fragmentation, climate change.
NTCA: Statutory body for tiger conservation.
mainsPoints: Evaluate the success of Project Tiger over the past 52 years in conserving tiger populations and biodiversity in India.
Discuss the major challenges that continue to impede tiger conservation efforts in India, including human-wildlife conflict and habitat fragmentation.
Examine the role of community participation and technological interventions in strengthening wildlife protection and management.
Suggest sustainable strategies for mitigating human-wildlife conflict and ensuring the long-term survival of tigers and their ecosystems.
GS3.imageDescription: A majestic Bengal tiger in its natural habitat, symbolizing conservation success.
GS3.trivia: The tiger is India's national animal. The first tiger census in India was conducted in 1972, estimating only around 1,800 tigers.

GS3.headline: Government Announces New Scheme for Promoting Organic Farming and Sustainable Agriculture Practices
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Agriculture and Farmers Welfare launched a new national scheme, 'Paramparagat Krishi Vikas Yojana Plus' (PKVY+), to promote organic and natural farming.
• The scheme provides financial assistance, technical support, and market linkages to farmers transitioning to sustainable agricultural methods.
• Emphasis is placed on cluster-based organic farming, certification, and value addition of organic produce.
• The initiative aims to reduce chemical fertilizer use, improve soil health, enhance biodiversity, and increase farmers' income through premium pricing.
• Training and capacity building programs will be conducted for farmers, with a focus on integrating traditional knowledge with modern sustainable practices.
gsSubTopics: Major crops-cropping patterns in various parts of the country, different types of irrigation and irrigation system storage, transport and marketing of agricultural produce and issues and related constraints
e-technology in the aid of farmers
Food processing and related industries in India- scope and significance, location, upstream and downstream requirements, supply chain management
prelimsPoints: PKVY+: New scheme to promote organic and natural farming.
Parent scheme: Paramparagat Krishi Vikas Yojana (PKVY) launched in 2015.
Objectives: Reduce chemical use, improve soil health, enhance biodiversity, increase farmer income.
Key features: Financial assistance, technical support, market linkages, cluster-based approach.
Benefits: Environmentally friendly, healthier food, higher returns for farmers.
mainsPoints: Discuss the rationale behind the government's renewed focus on organic and natural farming, considering environmental sustainability and farmer welfare.
Analyze the potential of schemes like 'Paramparagat Krishi Vikas Yojana Plus' in transforming Indian agriculture towards more sustainable practices.
Examine the challenges in scaling up organic farming in India, including certification, market access, and farmer adoption.
Suggest policy measures to integrate traditional agricultural knowledge with modern scientific methods for promoting sustainable farming systems.
GS3.imageDescription: A farmer tending to organic crops in a field, with healthy soil visible.
GS3.trivia: Sikkim was declared India's first fully organic state in 2016. Natural farming, often called 'chemical-free farming', is gaining traction as a low-cost, sustainable alternative.

GS3.headline: AI-Powered Diagnostics for Early Disease Detection: India's Progress in Health Tech Innovation
GS3.explainer:
• India is making significant strides in leveraging Artificial Intelligence (AI) for early and accurate disease diagnosis, particularly in remote areas.
• New AI-powered diagnostic tools are being deployed in primary health centers for conditions like diabetic retinopathy, tuberculosis, and certain cancers.
• These innovations aim to overcome the shortage of specialized medical professionals and improve healthcare accessibility.
• Government-backed initiatives and private sector collaborations are driving research and development in AI algorithms for medical imaging and predictive analytics.
• Ethical considerations, data privacy, and regulatory frameworks for AI in healthcare remain key areas of focus to ensure responsible adoption.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Achievements of Indians in science & technology; indigenization of technology and developing new technology
prelimsPoints: AI in healthcare: Used for early disease detection, diagnostics, predictive analytics.
Target diseases: Diabetic retinopathy, tuberculosis, certain cancers.
Benefit: Addresses shortage of specialists, improves accessibility, especially in rural areas.
Challenges: Ethical concerns, data privacy, regulatory frameworks.
Government initiatives: Ayushman Bharat Digital Mission (ABDM) promotes digital health.
mainsPoints: Discuss the transformative potential of Artificial Intelligence in revolutionizing disease diagnostics and improving healthcare outcomes in India.
Analyze the challenges and ethical considerations associated with the widespread adoption of AI in the Indian healthcare system, particularly concerning data privacy and bias.
Examine how AI-powered solutions can address the healthcare disparities and shortage of medical specialists in rural and remote regions of India.
Suggest policy recommendations for fostering innovation in health tech while ensuring robust regulatory oversight and public trust.
GS3.imageDescription: A doctor interacting with a holographic display showing medical data and AI algorithms.
GS3.trivia: The global AI in healthcare market is projected to grow significantly, with applications ranging from drug discovery to personalized medicine.

GS3.headline: Smart Cities 2.0: Focus on Climate Resilience and Green Urban Infrastructure in Next Phase
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Housing and Urban Affairs announced the conceptual framework for 'Smart Cities 2.0', emphasizing climate resilience and green infrastructure.
• This next phase of the Smart Cities Mission will prioritize adaptation to climate change, sustainable resource management, and reduction of urban carbon footprint.
• Projects will focus on developing green buildings, efficient public transport, renewable energy integration, and robust waste management systems.
• Increased citizen participation and data-driven governance will be central to planning and implementing smart city initiatives.
• Funding mechanisms will encourage public-private partnerships and innovative financing models for sustainable urban development.
gsSubTopics: Infrastructure: Energy, Ports, Roads, Airports, Railways etc.
Investment models
Urbanization, their problems and their remedies
prelimsPoints: Smart Cities Mission (original): Launched in 2015 to promote sustainable and inclusive cities.
Smart Cities 2.0: Next phase with focus on climate resilience and green infrastructure.
Key areas: Green buildings, public transport, renewable energy, waste management.
Funding: PPPs and innovative financing models.
Objective: Sustainable urban development, climate adaptation.
mainsPoints: Discuss how the 'Smart Cities 2.0' initiative, with its focus on climate resilience, addresses the growing environmental challenges faced by Indian urban centers.
Analyze the role of green infrastructure and sustainable urban planning in mitigating the urban carbon footprint and enhancing quality of life.
Examine the challenges in implementing climate-resilient urban projects, including funding, technology adoption, and citizen engagement.
Suggest innovative strategies for promoting public-private partnerships and community participation in achieving sustainable smart city development goals.
GS3.imageDescription: A futuristic city skyline with green spaces, solar panels, and modern infrastructure.
GS3.trivia: The Smart Cities Mission initially aimed to develop 100 smart cities across India. The concept includes features like smart governance, smart mobility, and smart environment.

GS3.headline: India's Chip Design Ecosystem Witnesses Significant Growth with New Investment Policies
GS3.explainer:
• The Ministry of Electronics and Information Technology (MeitY) reported substantial growth in India's semiconductor chip design ecosystem.
• New investment policies, including incentives under the India Semiconductor Mission (ISM), have attracted global and domestic players.
• The growth is seen in areas like VLSI design, embedded systems, and AI chip development, positioning India as a global design hub.
• Government is focusing on talent development through academic collaborations and skill-building programs to meet industry demands.
• The initiative is crucial for reducing India's dependence on chip imports and enhancing strategic autonomy in critical technology.
gsSubTopics: Science and Technology- developments and their applications and effects in everyday life
Indigenization of technology and developing new technology
prelimsPoints: India Semiconductor Mission (ISM): Government initiative to build a semiconductor ecosystem.
Focus: Chip design, manufacturing, packaging.
Growth areas: VLSI design, embedded systems, AI chips.
Incentives: PLI schemes, design-linked incentive (DLI) schemes.
Goal: Reduce import dependence, achieve strategic autonomy.
mainsPoints: Analyze the factors contributing to the growth of India's chip design ecosystem and its significance for the 'Atmanirbhar Bharat' initiative.
Discuss the challenges involved in establishing a complete semiconductor manufacturing ecosystem in India, from design to fabrication.
Examine the role of government policies and incentives, such as the India Semiconductor Mission, in attracting investments and fostering innovation.
Evaluate the geopolitical implications of India's push for semiconductor self-reliance in the context of global supply chain disruptions.
GS3.imageDescription: A close-up of a microchip with intricate circuitry.
GS3.trivia: The global semiconductor industry is highly concentrated, with Taiwan and South Korea being major players in manufacturing, while the US dominates design.
